11 min read
Just now

--

In December 2025, the US bet on deregulation to win the AI race while China built the world’s most detailed AI rulebook. Six months later, both approaches are being tested in real time.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One Signature, Fifty Fights
On December 11, 2025, President Trump signed an executive order with a bureaucratic name and an ambitious mission: Ensuring a National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence. CNBC’s Deirdre Bosa summed up the market’s read in a single headline: the US prioritizes speed over safety, as China takes the lead in regulation.
That framing sounds backwards at first. America, land of lawsuits and compliance departments, choosing lessregulation? China, a state known for tight information control, becoming the world’s AI rule-writer? But that’s the direction both governments chose, and understanding why explains most of what’s happening in AI policy right now, including things that look unrelated, like the export-control drama of June 2026.
An executive order, for anyone outside US politics, is a directive the President issues to federal agencies without needing Congress. It can’t create new laws, but it can tell every agency in the executive branch what to prioritize, and that alone can reshape an industry.
This one set a single sentence as national policy: the US will “sustain and enhance the United States’ global AI dominance through a minimally burdensome national policy framework for AI.” The word choices are deliberate and tell you the theory of the case: in the administration’s view, America’s decisive advantage in AI is the pace of its private sector, and the biggest risk to that advantage is compliance drag. Speed, on this theory, is the safety strategy, because falling behind a strategic rival is framed as the greater danger. Reasonable people disagree with that theory, and this article will get to them, but it is a coherent position, not an oversight.
And the obstacle the order targets first isn’t a foreign rival. It’s regulatory divergence among America’s own fifty states.
The Patchwork Problem
Here’s the situation the order was reacting to. The US has no comprehensive federal AI law. Congress has tried and failed repeatedly; the only standalone federal AI statute on the books is the TAKE IT DOWN Act, a narrow law about AI-generated intimate imagery. Into that vacuum, the states charged.
The numbers are genuinely wild. Over 1,000 AI-related bills were introduced across US states in 2025 alone, and by March 2026 lawmakers in 45 states had filed more than 1,500 AI bills, already exceeding all of 2024. Colorado enacted a comprehensive AI Act modeled loosely on the EU’s approach, regulating algorithmic discrimination (when an automated system produces unfair outcomes for protected groups in hiring, lending, housing and the like). California passed a package covering deepfakes, watermarking and frontier-model transparency. Texas, Illinois, Utah and New York all moved.
If you’re an AI startup, this really is painful: fifty potentially different rulebooks for one product that streams identically to every state. The order calls this the “patchwork” problem, and it’s a legitimate grievance that even critics of the order concede.
The administration’s answer is what one law firm dubbed the “One Rule” approach: one light federal standard, with several mechanisms directed at state laws that conflict with it:

• Litigation. The Attorney General was directed to create an AI Litigation Task Force whose sole responsibility is challenging state AI laws in court, arguing they unconstitutionally regulate interstate commerce or are preempted by federal law. Attorney General Pam Bondi formally established it on January 9, 2026, working with White House AI advisor David Sacks to identify which laws to challenge.

• Funding conditions. The Commerce Department was told to condition state access to portions of the $42 billion BEAD broadband program on states not enforcing AI laws the department deems “onerous.”

• Agency interpretation. The FTC and FCC were directed to explore reading existing federal statutes in ways that preempt state AI rules, including a theory that state anti-discrimination requirements force models to alter their “truthful outputs” and therefore conflict with federal deception law.

• A legislative proposal. The White House would draft a federal framework for Congress that preempts state laws, carving out child safety, data-center infrastructure and state procurement.
The order cites one state law by name: Colorado’s AI Act is called out explicitly as a law that, in the order’s characterization, could force models to produce false results to avoid disparate impacts on protected groups. Colorado’s defenders read the same statute the opposite way, as basic anti-discrimination protection extended to automated decisions. That disagreement, over whether bias mitigation is consumer protection or compelled distortion, is the philosophical core of the whole federal-state fight.
One thing the order cannot do, and this is where a lot of casual commentary gets it wrong: an executive order does not overturn state law. Only Congress or the courts can do that. Colorado’s law took effect in 2026 anyway. California’s rules are live. Companies still have to comply with all of it while the constitutional fight plays out, which means the order’s immediate effect was, ironically, more uncertainty, not less.
Meanwhile, in Beijing: The Rulebook Nobody Expected
Now flip to the other half of the CNBC headline, the part that raises eyebrows: China taking the lead in regulation.
This isn’t spin. It’s measurable. China issued as many national AI requirements in the first half of 2025 as it did in the previous three years combined, according to the consultancy Concordia AI, and Nature ran an editorial in December 2025 titled, with no irony, “China is leading the world on AI governance.”
What does China’s rulebook actually contain? A layered system built since 2022:

• Algorithm registration. Companies must register recommendation algorithms with regulators and keep them “transparent, secure and controllable.”

• Pre-deployment review. Generative AI services open to the public must undergo safety assessments and register with authorities before launch. In the US, no such gate exists; you can ship a frontier model to a hundred million people on a Tuesday.

• Mandatory content labeling. Since September 1, 2025, every piece of AI-generated content in China must carry labels: explicit ones users can see, and implicit ones embedded in file metadata (hidden information inside the file itself, like a digital fingerprint). A national standard, GB 45438–2025, specifies exactly how, down to watermark placement diagrams for images, video and audio.

• Ethics review. Draft measures from August 2025 require ethics committees and risk assessments for AI that significantly affects human behavior, emotions or health.

• A global play. At the 2025 World AI Conference, China released a Global AI Governance Action Plan and proposed a World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization to coordinate AI rules internationally, a seat the US left conspicuously empty.
Two honest caveats, because this comparison is easy to romanticize in either direction. First, China’s “safety” regulation is inseparable from state control: the labeling and traceability rules that fight fraud also make every piece of content attributable, which serves censorship as much as consumer protection. Chinese officials, as one USC law scholar put it, want AI that can’t rock political stability but can still drive growth. Second, regulating content is not the same as regulating catastrophic risk: the Future of Life Institute’s safety index actually scores prominent Chinese AI companies lower than Western ones on addressing catastrophic misuse.
So the honest summary isn’t “China good, US bad.” It’s that the two countries chose opposite default settings. China: regulate first, everything traceable, the state approves before you ship. The US: ship first, dominate, and treat regulation itself as the risk.

United States (Dec 2025 order) China (2022–2025 framework) Federal/national AI law None comprehensive; EO policy only Layered binding rules since 2022, draft national AI Law circulating Pre-launch review None (voluntary 30-day review added June 2026) Mandatory safety assessment + registration AI content labeling No federal rule; state laws under attack Mandatory explicit + implicit labels, national standard Posture toward internal regulators Sues its own states Provinces implement central rules Stated goal” Global AI dominance,” minimal burden Development and control, “AI Plus” 90% penetration by 2030 of frontier models Rule-writer, proposes world AI body
Two Orders, Six Months Apart
Here’s where this story earns its place in 2026 rather than staying a December 2025 news item.
The December order treated regulation as the primary risk to American AI. Then, in June 2026, the same administration ordered Anthropic to suspend access to its most advanced models worldwide over a reported cybersecurity exploit, gated OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 launch behind a government review, and signed a new executive order asking frontier labs to give the government access to models 30 days before release.
How you read those two orders side by side depends on where you sit, and both readings deserve a fair hearing.
Critics see a contradiction. The administration spent December arguing that safety rules slow innovation, then executed the most drastic AI intervention any democracy has made, improvised through export-control law rather than legislation, with no published legal explanation even after a bipartisan group of House members demanded one. Brad Carson of the AI-safety group Public First argued the episode shows the need for clear regulations, calling the current approach “ad hoc, personalized, opaque, possibly lawless.” On this view, declining to write AI rules in advance didn’t eliminate governance; it guaranteed that when governance became unavoidable, it arrived as emergency improvisation.
The administration and its supporters see no contradiction at all. In their framing, the two orders address different problems with different tools: December was about economic regulation, the compliance burden of fifty divergent state regimes on commercial AI products, while June was a national security action against a specific capability risk in specific frontier models. A government can coherently believe that hiring-algorithm disclosure mandates are wasteful and that a model capable of discovering critical-infrastructure vulnerabilities warrants export controls. Deregulating commerce and controlling weapons-grade capability, on this view, are complementary halves of one dominance strategy, not a reversal. And deregulation advocates level their own critique from the opposite direction: Neil Chilson of the Abundance Institute warned that arbitrary use of export-control authority will make companies slow-walk new models, depriving the public of new tools — an argument that the June actions, not the December order, are the mistake.
What’s not in dispute is the practical consequence for everyone outside the US: whatever the doctrine, allies and customers watched American models become subject to sudden government-directed access changes, and several accelerated sovereign AI programs in response. Whether that trade-off was worth the security benefit is precisely the debate now running in Washington, Brussels and Delhi.
Where the Fight Stands Now
As of mid-2026, the December order is machinery in motion with few finished products:

• December 11, 2025 — Executive order signed; Colorado’s AI Act named as a target

• January 9, 2026 — DOJ AI Litigation Task Force formally established

• February 1, 2026 — Colorado’s high-risk AI regime takes effect any
